title: The Second Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: June 14, 2020
Preacher: Richard Lawson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Second Sunday after Pentecost falls within Ordinary Time in the church calendar. The RCL readings for this Sunday typically include 1 Samuel 15:34-16:13 (David's anointing), Psalm 23, 2 Corinthians 5:6-17 (reconciliation in Christ), and Mark 4:26-34 (parables of the kingdom). This period follows the Pentecost season and emphasizes sustained Christian living and discipleship rather than major feast observances.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church had been actively engaged in racial justice conversations following the 2018 General Convention's commitments to address racism. Bishop Robert Wright of the Diocese of Colorado (serving since 2013) would have been navigating the denomination's response to the national racial reckoning underway in mid-June 2020. Saint John's Cathedral, as the diocesan seat, held particular significance in representing the church's institutional stance during this moment.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced significant civil unrest in the days immediately preceding this sermon. Large protests in downtown Denver and the Capitol Hill neighborhood occurred on June 1-2 following George Floyd's death, with some property damage and police response. By June 14, Denver remained under heightened tensions; the city had implemented curfews (lifted by early June) and conversations about police reform were dominant in local media and community spaces. Saint John's Cathedral location on Cheever Place in downtown Denver placed it at the symbolic and geographic center of these events.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS

George Floyd's death on May 25 in Minneapolis and the ensuing nationwide protests dominated the national conversation; by June 14, demonstrations had continued for nearly three weeks in all 50 states. President Trump's June 1 walk through Lafayette Square (forcibly cleared of protesters) and his threats of military deployment remained highly controversial. The debate over Confederate statues and monuments had intensified dramatically, with removals occurring across the country. Additionally, the COVID-19 pandemic was still the secondary but persistent news story, with case numbers rising as states reopened.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The UK was in the midst of similar racial justice protests following George Floyd's death, with demonstrations in London and other cities intensifying anti-racism messaging. Brazil was experiencing one of the world's worst COVID-19 outbreaks with accelerating case numbers and political controversy surrounding President Bolsonaro's response. Israel was preparing for formal annexation discussions regarding West Bank settlements, with international diplomatic tensions rising.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The United States was experiencing acute collective trauma and moral reckoning regarding systemic racism, police violence, and structural inequality. The phrase "Black Lives Matter" had moved from activist slogan to mainstream institutional acknowledgment, with corporations, nonprofits, and churches issuing statements. There was profound generational anxiety, particularly among younger Americans. Sports were absent from the cultural landscape due to COVID-19 suspensions, removing a traditional unifying element. The cultural mood was urgent, divided, and spiritually searching—churches faced particular pressure to speak prophetically while their congregations were themselves fractured along political and racial lines.
